answer:
Both authors are in the corpus and directly address slavery, but their objections are structurally distinct — they share a common surface (ancient slavery produced lasting civilizational damage) while targeting different domains.

**Comte's strongest formulation** (charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99, ch. 1, paragraph p-e537a3): 'The order of things in the midst of which they were placed did not permit them to go back to the true principles of laws and of morals, and to follow their consequences. They could not have founded the rights and duties of each individual on the very nature of man, without putting their doctrines in opposition with their practices, and without proclaiming the illegitimacy of slavery. They were thus under the necessity of admitting certain principles which they made the foundation of their rights, and of which they could not have demonstrated the truth.' Summarized at p-1f084b: 'while we are led, by the nature of our social state, to make the last vestiges of servitude disappear, we steep ourselves in the doctrines of domestic slavery, of feudal servitude, of military despotism... the doctrines of the ones and the others are still full of life; we make of them an essential part of the teaching of laws and of morals; we learn them from our childhood in our schools; we invoke them in our courts of justice.' Comte's objection is jurisprudential and epistemological: because the ancient slave-owning jurists (Aristotle, Plato, Cicero, Papinian, Paul, Ulpian) could not ground rights in human nature without invalidating slavery, they built law on undemonstrated, arbitrary principles. That corrupted foundation was inherited wholesale by modern legal systems.

**Dunoyer's strongest formulation** (charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce, ch. 7, paragraphs p-2c2f36, p-e40a65, p-b11ee4, p-e73720): The mechanism is incentive destruction in both directions. On the slave side (citing Ferguson/Bentham): 'the fear of punishment, far from exciting him to show his power, counsels him on the contrary to disguise it: he would put himself in the wrong by a work of supererogation; he would, in showing his capacity, only raise the measure of his duties. An inverse ambition is thus established, and industry aspires to descend rather than to ascend.' On the master side: 'Wherever men can compel others to work for them, it is very rare that they instruct themselves, that they become industrious, that they render themselves capable of anything useful. Inactivity of the mind is the consequence of that of the body: with whip in hand, one is dispensed from intelligence.' The result for Rome: agricultural decline ('every time the agronomists of antiquity complain of the diminution of wheat in Italy, they do not fail to attribute this decrease in territorial wealth to the introduction of servile exploitation'), population collapse, and the virtual absence of manufactures. General conclusion (p-b11ee4): 'everywhere [slavery] has the effect of stupefying and depraving the populations it supports, of opposing the progress of their industry, of their private morals, of their social habits.' Dunoyer's objection is economic and motivational.

**Are they making the same case?** No. The shared surface is: 'slavery produces harm that radiates beyond the enslaved themselves and persists into later civilization.' But the specific mechanism and domain differ sharply. Comte targets the jurisprudential legacy: slavery made it structurally impossible for ancient legal thinkers to ground law in natural rights, so all subsequent legal inheritance is built on unverifiable arbitrary principles — a corruption of legal epistemology. Dunoyer targets industrial capacity: slavery destroys the incentive structures required for productive development in both master and slave, producing stupefaction, stagnation, and eventual material collapse — a corruption of economic motivation. Comte's damage propagates through legal texts and institutions across centuries; Dunoyer's damage propagates through the degradation of human capacities within slave societies and analogously in the Americas. They are two genuinely different objections that happen to share the same target.
